sentences.text: I mentioned that the principal simply couldn't expel students, and some students came in really harassed the Jew students.
In fact, by that time, only-- if I'm not mistaken, there were only one or two Jewish students left-- already had left Germany.
But we left, and I went to a Jewish school.
Happened to be the reform school of the reform congregation.
Still part of the overall congregation, but, uh, it was reform.
Other schools conservative.
One, [INAUDIBLE] school, had very Zionist leanings, but they went to the school-- Sukkoth.
Joseph Lehmann school.
Joseph Lehmann was one of the prominent reform rabbi.
And there were boys and girls together, and then we continued, ah, our schooling.
Yes.
Yes, exactly what happened.
No, that came later.
I don't recall.
I know at one time, of course, but I left before that.
We didn't wait till [INAUDIBLE].
I would say I started-- that this was probably '35, '36.
I remember when the Olympic games came, I already was there.
It started just about that time.
Absolutely.
Actually, uh, it was quite strange to be together with girls, but otherwise they were very good teachers.
But, um, otherwise, actually, once in school, we forgot everything else.
Sometimes we liked it.
Sometimes we didn't like it.
It was still exactly the same like in other school, but, um, otherwise we were quite happy there.
Yes, definitely.
They had to.
All the [INAUDIBLE] with the Jewish schools, they were still checked-- the subjects.
It was a kind of-- we called it class book.
In there, the teacher had to put in, number one, who was late, who was absent, what was covered, and, uh, homework.
Therefore, it could be, at the end of the session, if-- if the book was audited, you could see exactly what had been covered.
It was quite strict.
Yes.
It was a Jewish school but still had to follow the rules and regulation laid down by the overall school system.
Yes, it taught Jewish subjects, but, uh, certain things had to be, uh, taught.
Yes.
Actually, some-- there was one difficulty, actually.
There were enough high school-- Jewish high school teachers, but not, uh, as many grade school teachers.
And I believe there were some teachers, uh, who were actually original teachers, very good.
But, uh, actually, I remember-- oops, that's, um-- were not teachers, but, uh, most of the time were teachers that I remembered in grade school, the very best.
Later on, of course, the high school teachers were more than enough.
Yes.
Definitely.
Some, actually, in small towns, for many reasons, there was no Jewish school.
They came, that's correct.
They lived, uh-- I remember particularly later on in high school, there were several who lived, uh-- had room and board with some family and, uh, went to school there.
This was exactly.
That's correct.
In some cities, like Hamburg, for instance, they had still Jewish schools, bigger than Hamburg.
I know for sure Hamburg, and I assume there were other cities too.
But yes, that's correct.
[SIGHS] I did not encounter it, at least not-- not at the school.
What happened at the time, if somebody looked really Jewish, that's probably what happened.
Ah, look at him.
Yes.
But I, personally, was very fortunate.
Aside from what happened at the-- at the public school at the end, I did not-- I don't recall anything.
'35, '36, [INAUDIBLE].
Yes.
It started out, you already have signs at restaurants, Jews are not permitted, or some-- I remember one sign-- childrens-- children of Israel, turn back.
See, but not permitted.
Very often, people didn't mean it, but you still didn't like to go in there.
And you actually had the benches started out for Jews only.
And you had certainly very vicious anti-Semitic paper, They had public displays.
That happened, yes.
Yes.
Correct.
That's good.
Actually, they had special, uh, stands for that.
Frankly, I don't like-- I didn't go to find out.
I gave them a wide berth.
But some people were standing there, but I have a feeling not as many as they hoped it would be.
Yes.
No, very little.
Yes.
Yes.
It shall be imminent.
What can we do?
They talked about the quotas, United States, and where to go, and what might happen, and should we go.
Then of course, you always heard, but that-- yeah, this can't be.
Remember, the Germans, the uh-- the land of the poets and the thinkers, they wouldn't do something like this.
They are civilized here.
Um-- Yes, some people predicted, foresaw it, but others don't.
Correct.
Well, he got a pension.
Pension was somewhat more favorable for those who were, uh, from the so-called front camp or front soldiers.
But otherwise, we had to-- the money was quite tight.
We had to move to a small apartment.
But fortunately, my aunt-- my mother's sister-- took care of me.
From socks to caps, that part-- I remember going out, going buying shoes or clothes.
Afterwards, we went to a-- for some cafe, had some stuff with whipped cream.
I remember distinctly.
I didn't like the part of, uh, buying clothes, but what happened afterwards I enjoyed very much.
Yes.
I really don't know.
This I don't know.
I know that, uh, still at the beginning, my father we had still contact with, particularly non-Jewish colleagues.
But bit by bit, they felt they couldn't be seen and that, uh-- I remember one time they came with flowers and candy, and that was the last visit, unfortunately.
So.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Actually, I remember, uh, once my father's friend suggested they go to South Africa.
He would lend them money.
But my father felt, well, leaving Germany, accepting money, he didn't want to do it.
And the friend then left for what that time was no Israel, but Palestine.
Yes, probably my father at the beginning didn't quite believe it would happen either, but he realized it.
And then he try to get an affidavit, which what we have to have.
But by the time the affidavit came through, it was too late.
It must have been right before '38, '39, because any earlier, it's-- I believe it was '39.
United States would not get in the war.
And that-- but it was impossibility to get out any more/ I remember it.
He went to-- remember he went-- he went to the American consulate.
That was the time, uh, he took off his decorations, because--
--because I cannot-- is why, I ask.
It would be tactless to go to the, um, American embassy or consulate with decorations I earned in a fight against the United States.
Yes, yes, yes.
That usually-- small, um, lapel.
He got Iron Cross, and then he got also a decoration, a so-called, uh, Honor Cross for all, uh, people that served at the front got that.
And lo and behold in 1935, there came a letter with the name of the Fuehrer and Chancellor, you're hereby awarded this particular thing.
It was in 1935 when Jew or no Jew, the regulation stated, everyone who fought, was at the front will receive this, uh, so-called Honor Cross.
He had his.
Absolutely.
Absolutely.
He lost his job in '33.
In '35, he got that.
No, they knew it quite well.
In fact, it said, to-- to the judge, out of office more than 80 other deans, they knew quite well.
They gave him the full title.
They knew.
But no, it was right.
With a swastika right there.
So he had it coming, he was entitled to get it, and he was going to get it.
And Jewish or not-Jewish, they had the regulation that-- that everybody gets it.
It didn't mention Jews, therefore, everybody got it.
He belonged, I know, to the Social Democratic Party.
He wasn't overly active, but he was interested in it.
I believe that what happened-- of course, I don't know, I was told-- at the beginning, no, no, that couldn't be in Germany.
That can't happen.
Civilized.
And Hitler, a temporary aberration.
Just wait till you see what he's really like, then they'll see.
But by the time they realized, it was too late.
He was entrenched.
That was it.
I think nobody really, uh, realized what happened.
In retrospect, I feel-- it's my own opinion-- had people read the Nazi newspaper, had they read-- there were two newspapers, one a cultivated one, was the That was the official Nazi.
And the-- Goebbels [INAUDIBLE] published it.
And then there was one, That was the SS.
Yes, I think so.
Of course.
There were two newspapers, one local and one more for Germany as such.
I actually didn't read them.
Occasionally, I read the local newspaper, but I-- frankly, only once in a while.
And I heard-- heard people talk, yes.
Uh-- try to, uh, to go to various countries, to China, Brazil, Uruguay, and so on.
You knew that people try to get out.
I really don't know.
This I don't know.
I was too young to actually, uh, go and to read it.
Right.
Well, the parades was always there, but not before beginning of the war.
I remember close to 1939, just before the war, you could see quite a bit of, uh, trains, with tanks, and so on.
And actually it was quite obvious that something would happen, you see.
We didn't know what.
But, uh, you could see it.
That was the beginning, uh, of the actual war.
Yes, it was quite something.
In fact, all the signs disappeared.
Everything.
You could sit everywhere.
You could do anything you wanted.
And I was being told, and probably I believe it, that people were instructed, be very careful.
No anti-Semitic remark.
Be very polite to everyone.
Because you didn't know, somebody might look Jewish, but, uh, who knows?
Was from Italy or you know.
And, uh, I remember they got brand new, um, elevated trains.
And, uh, everything was absolutely-- streets were clean.
Absolutely everything was just organized beautiful.
And you could go everywhere.
At that time already, um, they didn't like to see you in certain movies- movie theaters and, uh, opera houses.
It wasn't officially, that came a little bit later, but, uh, probably some people didn't.
But the general population was not already anti-Semitic, but already enough people were looking for troubles.
Say, oh, what you're doing here?
But at that time, you could do-- go everywhere and could do anything you wanted.
So the Olympics was kind of like being in the eye of a storm.
Absolutely.
The moment it was over, the signs were back again.
Everything was again, you see.
Well, they come, for instance, with special identification cards.
You had to have a special identification card, and had to with the name of Israel for men and Sara  for women.
If you had a Jewish name like, uh, well, [INAUDIBLE] that time was considered a Jewish name.
But unless your first name was really Jewish, obviously so, you had to-- Israel.
And you had to had-- got a number and, uh, your so-called identification number.
And wherever any of your signature had to be, uh, your name with Israel and the number of your identification card.
Israel, absolutely.
It was suddenly.
One day it was Fritz Israel.
That was it.
Whether you liked it or not, that was it.
Or Sara with a woman.
[INAUDIBLE].
Well, Israel is my Hebrew name anyway.
Therefore, I'm afraid it's only made it official.
It did not particularly disturb me.
I remember we got it, we had to report to a barracks, military barracks, which was used to empty the-- in order to issue those cards.
But, um, then, of course, that might not be then known, one of the Jewish stores it had to put the name of the owner in big-- it was that tall letters right on the window.
And of course, then came the Kristallnacht and they knew exactly what windows to break.
No.
I have no.
No, I was fortunate, I didn't witness.
I witnessed a name calling somewhere, but [COUGHS] no beatings actually.
I very fortunate that, uh, I never saw it.
Yes, actually, they started out exclusion from movie theaters and it became then official.
Right after the Olympic games, it became official, no movie theaters, operas, and so on.
But, uh, even there we had-- what you call, the Jewish Kulturbund.
I would call the cultural association.
We went there, and I remember we went to concerts and some oratorios.
Even had, uh-- we saw a movie there.
OK, we couldn't go-- you used to go Sunday afternoons to a special movie, uh, performance.
We couldn't do this anymore.
But on the other hand, unless you looked very Jewish, at that time, nobody, uh, asked you.
But I personally didn't do it.
And at that time, it started bit by bit.
No.
There were two sport clubs in Berlin.
One, the Zionist Bar-Kochba.
And the other was, um, they call it-- they had no Zionist leanings.
They were more or less, uh-- they were-- more or less founded by a particular-- called it a Jewish firm, soldiers.
Jewish front soldiers and they were actually, uh--
Not paramilitary, but I believe they were more, um, I guess, they considered themself Jewish Germans.
They were quite mindful of their Jewi-- of their German heritage.
And, um, it was what was the official association of Jewish front soldiers.
Quite-- in fact, what actually, um, can tell you their state of mind.
In the obituaries, in the paper they had the special, they didn't say died, but gone to the great army.
You see, stuff like this.
My father, although he was a front soldier, did not like them.
And I went to Bar-Kochba, the one with the Zionist leaning.
Absolutely.
Yes, yes.
Yes, they had Jewish-- there were public summer camps, I remember.
Went to summer camps at the Baltic, I remember.
We were all sitting in the train already, and suddenly somebody coming running, waving a piece of paper there.
What's going on?
Well, he just got a telegram that the camp at the Baltic was taken over by the local Nazi party.
Well, didn't go.
But next year went to another camp in the mountains.
Yes.
No, no, actually, but I had-- I-- frankly, I had my grandfather's violin.
Of course, had to be used, therefore, I was given violin lessons.
I wasn't very good, I assure you.
The poor people.
My ear wasn't good enough.
But I took violin lessons.
And then actually later on, my poor violin teacher was deported and then my violin burned.
But, uh, yes, I, uh, had violin lessons.
Yes, but that happened, actually, after-- I would say, the Kristallnacht, once-- the deportation actually happened in 1940, I would say.
The war started in '39.
The deportation started in 1940.
Then we became aware of it.
Well, yes.
That happened actually after the Kristallnacht.
The-- but that was in '38.
Yeah, that--
Actually, the Kristallnacht too.
Once the Kristallnacht, Jews were not permitted to have any cars.
Physicians and dentists were only allowed to treat Jews.
And actually, it was a catalyst.
Once, after the Kristallnacht, the-- let's say, they really got going.
Yes.
Absolutely, absolutely.
In the morning, I go out and suddenly I realize lots of glass on the floor.
Then I suddenly realize the glass came from the Jewish stores.
And then it dawned on me why they had the white, uh, signs on there.
This was all planned and was just waiting for something like this.
Hadn't been the murder of the, uh, secretary in Paris or the embassy secretary, but something else would happen [INAUDIBLE].
Yes.
Definitely, yes.
The population, the people, who didn't do it.
In fact, they were quite taken aback.
Definitely.
This was a group that-- uh-- oh, some gangs were actually-- that was correct.
Of course didn't talk to them, but, uh, I only can tell what the non-Jewish relatives, of course, they were appalled.
Some people, for no other reason, this was again, order is very important in Germany.
How could it happen?
People go around breaking windows and, um, lay fire to synagogues.
Well, that's unheard of.
It was not very helpful.
See, I think they didn't realize what would happen.
The Kristallnacht did not have the expected effect.
No.
I don't think, no.
Wasn't aware of what happened.
[INAUDIBLE] for instance, there was some-- one synagogue was located, uh, close to apartment buildings.
They came and wanted to burn it.
There was a policeman standing there.
Are you out of your mind?
If you do this, you will cause [INAUDIBLE] flames.
I would say, people were not standing there and laughing and applauding, not at all.
Maybe in some cases.
I understand, however, much worse outside Berlin.
Much worse.
In Berlin, too, it was a big city.
Many, uh, people of foreign countries, diplomats, I guess, realized that, uh-- however, what they did, arrested many Jews.
Many of my teachers that was in the Jewish high school, uh, were taken in.
I remember I took home a little letter.
Because of special circumstances, the report cards will be late this year.
Everybody knew what the special circumstances were.
This I remember.
And even some distant relative was arrested too and [INAUDIBLE] Sachsenhausen.
Yes, definitely.
In fact, he had been arrested-- he was in a smaller town about 50 miles east of Berlin.
It was much worse there.
He was arrested and brought to Sachsenhausen.
And then he came and he was released.
He came to us, I remember.
And with no hair.
And, uh, what they did to the clothes, the regular clothes, I guess they disinfect them, and looked dreadful, all wrinkled and so on.
I knew him distinctly.
And of course, the teachers that came back.
No, they didn't.
Very little.
I think so.
I think even then, the most, uh, people who doubted it, finally realized if that is possible in an orderly state.
In some way, perhaps it was still encouraging that the population as such didn't participate.
But then they couldn't do anything about it.
I think there was a bigger surprise, actually, that the population didn't go run around applauding, you see.
Perhaps some did, but what my personal experience, not at all.
